context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 7

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 8th April, 2024
Days Covered: 13 and 14 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Tree's Degrees [13 points]

Let T be a tree with n ≥ 2 vertices, of which k have degree at least 3.

(a) [8 pts] Prove by induction on n that T has at least k + 2 leaves.
    Hint: Pick a leaf.
(b) [5 pts] Prove the same result using the Handshake Lemma instead of induction.
    Hint: How many edges does a tree with n vertices have?


Problem 2. Froli-King [12 points]

(a) [6 pts] When moving on an 8×8 chessboard, a King can step to any of the ≤ 8 cells that are immediately adjacent to its current cell, either horizontally, vertically, or diagonally. It may be verified that there are 420 possible moves a King can make: 56 moves in each of the four compass directions (N, S, E, W), and 49 moves in each of the four diagonal directions (NE, NW, SE, SW).
Consider placing a King on an otherwise empty chessboard and performing a sequence of consecutive King moves. Say that this sequence is Frolicsome if it (a) starts and ends in the same cell, (b) never performs the same move twice, and (c) never performs a move that it has already done in the opposite direction. Conditions (b) and (c) can be said differently: if the king ever moves from cell c1 to cell c2, then he is not allowed to move from c1 to c2 OR from c2 to c1 for the rest of the walk.
Explain why there does not exist a Frolicsome sequence of King moves consisting of exactly 210 steps.
    Hint: Model this as a graph problem. What are the nodes and edges? Show that a 210-step Frolicsome sequence corresponds to an Euler tour of your graph.
(b) [6 pts] Show that there does exist a Frolicsome sequence of 196 King moves in this 8×8 board. (Show it exists, but don't actually construct it!)
    Hint: Find 14 edges to remove in order to make your graph Eulerian.


Problem 3. DAGs [12 points]

Answer the following questions about the dependency DAG shown in the diagram. Assume each node is a task that takes 1 second.

[Figure: a directed acyclic graph on nodes 1–12. Top row: 1 → 2 → 3, 4, 5, 6, with additional edges 2 → 6, 3 → 6, 5 → 6. Bottom row: 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, with edges 7 → 3, 8 → 3, 3 → 10, 9 → 10, 11 → 12, 10 → 12.]

(a) [2 pts] What is the largest chain in this DAG? If there is more than one, only give one.
(b) [2 pts] What is the largest antichain? (Again, give only one if there are more than one.) Prove there isn't a larger antichain.
(c) [2 pts] How much time would be required to complete all the tasks with a single processor?
(d) [2 pts] How much time would be required to complete all the tasks if there are unlimited processors available?
(e) [2 pts] What is the smallest number p of processors that would still allow completion of all the tasks in optimal time? Show a schedule for p processors that completes in optimal time, and prove that p − 1 or fewer processors must take longer.
(f) [2 pts] What is a topological ordering of these tasks? If there is more than one, only give one.


Problem 4. Pokémon Constraints [13 points]

Ash is selecting a team of Pokémon to use in an upcoming tournament. Each of his Pokémon has a number of types, shown below.

| Pokémon          | Types        |
|------------------|--------------|
| Altaria          | Dragon, Flying |
| Bidoof           | Normal       |
| Chikorita        | Grass        |
| Dratini          | Dragon       |
| Eevee            | Normal       |
| Floragato        | Grass        |
| Grovyle          | Grass        |

Ash has several constraints he must satisfy while selecting his team. We model this task as a graph problem. Let the propositions A, B, C, D, E, F, G be "(Altaria, Bidoof, Chikorita, Dratini, Eevee, Floragato, Grovyle, respectively) is on Ash's team". These propositions and their negations are called literals. Ash's first constraint is that his team must include at least one of Altaria and Eevee, expressed as the 2-clause (A ∨ E).

The two literals in a 2-clause needn't be distinct, and they can be negated. Ash's next two constraints are that his team must include Eevee, and that it cannot include Eevee without Dratini, expressed as the 2-clauses (E ∨ E) and (NOT E ∨ D). Ash's next constraint is that his team must include exactly one of Bidoof and Chikorita, expressed (along with the previous three constraints) as a formula in 2-conjunctive normal form (2-CNF):
    φ1 := (A ∨ E) ∧ (E ∨ E) ∧ (NOT E ∨ D) ∧ (B ∨ C) ∧ (NOT B ∨ NOT C)

(a) [2 pts] Fill in the blanks in the following 2-CNF formula with literals to express the constraint that no two Pokémon on Ash's team may share a type.
    φ2 := (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _)
[The five blanks correspond to the five type-sharing pairs: the Dragon pair, the Normal pair, and the three Grass pairs.]

(b) [3 pts] We can now convert the constraint formula φ := φ1 ∧ φ2 into an implication graph H = (V, I). V := {A, B, C, D, E, F, G, NOT A, NOT B, NOT C, NOT D, NOT E, NOT F, NOT G} is the set of literals, and I encodes the 2-clauses of φ as implications. A 2-clause (u ∨ v) can be written in the logically equivalent form (NOT u ⇒ v) ∧ (NOT v ⇒ u). For every 2-clause (u ∨ v) of φ, I contains the edges (NOT u, v) and (NOT v, u). For instance, I contains the two edges (NOT A, E) and (NOT E, A) to encode (A ∨ E). Draw H or list each vertex's out-neighbors explicitly.
    Hint: H is planar and has 19 edges.

(c) [3 pts] Let ψ be a 2-CNF formula, and let u, v be literals in ψ. Prove that if ψ is true and the implication graph of ψ contains a path π from u to v, then u ⇒ v.
    Hint: Induction on path length.

(d) [3 pts] Identify the SCCs of H (there should be 8). Draw the condensation graph of H, or list each SCC's out-neighbors explicitly.

(e) [2 pts] Find a topological order on the SCCs of H, and identify the set T of literals v for which [NOT v] precedes [v] in this order. Setting these literals to True gives a team that satisfies all of Ash's constraints! What is T, and what is Ash's team?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i'm kinda stuck on PS7 for 6.1200 rn 😅 maybe Problem 1 first, esp the induction part about trees having at least k+2 leaves.
exchanges.tutor: Good choice to start there! Before we dive in, let's make sure the foundation is solid. When you do induction on n (the number of vertices), what's the first thing you need to establish?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting Problem 1a. I should use the Socratic method to help them begin thinking about the problem rather than scaffolding it for them. The hint in the problem says 'Pick a leaf' - I should get the student to think about what induction on n means here and what the base case looks like, before diving into the inductive step. I'll ask a targeted opening question to get them thinking.

exchanges.student: the base case, right? i think maybe n=2 since the problem says n≥2.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly. So what does a tree with n=2 look like, and what is k in that case? Does the inequality hold?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the base case as n=2. I should now push them to actually work through the base case rather than just naming it. I want them to think about what a tree with n=2 vertices looks like, what k would be in that case, and whether the inequality holds.

exchanges.student: it’s just 2 vertices with 1 edge, so both have degree 1. then k=0, and leaves =2, so 2≥k+2=2 works.
exchanges.tutor: Perfect, the base case checks out. Now for the inductive step — the hint says 'pick a leaf'. What happens to the tree if you remove a leaf?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly worked through the base case. Now I should move them to the inductive step, which is the harder and more interesting part. The hint says 'pick a leaf' - I should get them thinking about what happens when you remove a leaf from the tree, and what that does to n and k.

exchanges.student: i think it stays a tree on n-1 vertices, since removing a leaf shouldn’t disconnect the rest. is that the key move?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's the key move! Now think carefully: when you remove a leaf, what happens to its neighbor? Does k or the number of leaves necessarily change, and if so, how? Consider different cases based on the neighbor's degree.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that removing a leaf gives a tree on n-1 vertices. Now I need to push them to think more carefully about what removing a leaf does to k and the leaf count - this is the crux of the inductive step. There are different cases depending on the degree of the leaf's neighbor, and they need to think through those cases.

exchanges.student: i'm not fully seeing it yet tbh. if the neighbor had degree 2, it becomes a leaf; if it had bigger degree maybe k changes?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct! Try to be systematic — what are all the possible degrees the neighbor could have (remember, it's connected to the leaf, so its degree is at least 1), and for each case, what happens to both k and the number of leaves in the smaller tree?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track but needs to think through the cases more carefully. They've identified the degree-2 case and hinted at higher degree cases. I should push them to be more precise and systematic about all the cases: what are the possible degrees of the leaf's neighbor, and what happens to k and the leaf count in each case? The key cases are: neighbor has degree 2 (becomes a leaf, k unchanged), neighbor has degree 3 (loses a leaf but neighbor leaves k, since it drops to degree 2), and neighbor has degree >3 (k decreases by 1). I should guide them to enumerate these without giving the answer.

exchanges.student: ok so deg 1 would only happen if n=2 i think, so for induction maybe ignore that. then deg 2: removing the leaf makes neighbor become degree 1, so i lose one leaf but gain one leaf, and k stays same; deg 3: neighbor drops to 2 so k goes down by 1; deg ≥4: k stays same i think.
exchanges.tutor: Almost there, but double-check your deg≥4 case — if a neighbor has degree 4 and drops to 3, what happens to k? And for the deg 3 and deg≥4 cases, you haven't said what happens to the number of leaves — does it change?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is mostly on the right track but made an error in the deg≥4 case - if a neighbor had degree 4 and drops to degree 3, k stays the same (still ≥3). But if it had degree 3 and drops to degree 2, k decreases by 1. They also need to think about what happens to the leaf count in the deg 3 and deg≥4 cases. Let me push them to check the leaf count changes for those cases too, and also gently correct the deg≥4 case analysis.

exchanges.student: oh wait yeah, if it was 4 and goes to 3, it still counts in k, so k stays same. for leaves: in deg 3 or more, the neighbor doesn’t become a leaf, so i think total leaves drops by 1.
exchanges.tutor: Good, now you have all the pieces. Apply the inductive hypothesis to the smaller tree (n-1 vertices), then re-add the leaf and check: does the bound 'at least k+2 leaves' hold for the original tree in each case?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now correctly analyzed all cases. Now I should push them to actually apply the inductive hypothesis to each case and check that the conclusion follows. They need to apply IH to the smaller tree (n-1 vertices) and then re-add the leaf to see that the original tree satisfies the bound. This is the final synthesis step.

exchanges.student: i think so, but i'm mixing up old k vs new k lol. can u help me set up the two cases cleanly?
exchanges.tutor: That's a notation problem you can solve yourself! Try giving the smaller tree's values their own names — something like k' and leaves' — and write out what each equals in terms of the original k and leaf count. Then apply the IH. Give it a shot and show me what you get.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to set up the cases for them, which would be doing the work for them. I should not do that. Instead, I should encourage them to try writing it out themselves and remind them that distinguishing 'old k' from 'new k' just means using different notation. I can nudge them to define their notation clearly rather than handing them the setup.

exchanges.student: ok, let T' be after removing a leaf. if neighbor had deg 2, then k'=k and leaves(T')=leaves(T); if neighbor had deg at least 3, then leaves(T')=leaves(T)-1, and i think k' is k-1 only in the deg 3 case, otherwise k'=k.
exchanges.tutor: Good setup! Now apply the inductive hypothesis: T' has n-1 vertices, so leaves(T') ≥ k' + 2. Use this and your case analysis to conclude that leaves(T) ≥ k + 2 in each case. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly set up the cases with proper notation. Now they need to apply the inductive hypothesis to T' and then re-add the leaf to get back to T, checking the bound in each case. This is the final synthesis step - I should push them to do it.

exchanges.student: for deg 2, IH gives leaves(T)=leaves(T')≥k'+2=k+2. for deg 3, leaves(T)=leaves(T')+1≥(k-1)+2+1=k+2, and for deg ≥4, leaves(T)=leaves(T')+1≥k+2+1 so also works.
exchanges.tutor: Excellent — all three cases work out! You have a complete inductive proof. Make sure your writeup clearly states the inductive hypothesis, handles the base case, and walks through each case with this reasoning. Ready to tackle part (b) with the Handshake Lemma?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has successfully completed the inductive proof for all cases. This is correct and well-reasoned. I should affirm their work and encourage them to write it up cleanly as a formal proof. I can also nudge them toward part (b) if they're ready.
